subject: Physics
q: The activity of a radioactive material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>. If the half life of the material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days, after how many days the activity will become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>?
question: The activity of a radioactive material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>. If the half life of the material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days, after how many days the activity will become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>?
solution:
The activity of a radioactive sample at any time t is given by,

A=A02-tT12⇒2×10-5=2.56×10-3×2-t5⇒2t5=128⇒t5=7⇒t=35 days

subject: Physics
q:
A nucleus with $\mathrm{Z}=92$ emits the following in a sequence:
$\alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-} \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-}, \alpha, \beta^{+}, \beta^{+}, \alpha$. Then $\mathrm{Z}$ of the resulting nucleus is
question:
A nucleus with $\mathrm{Z}=92$ emits the following in a sequence:
$\alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-} \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-}, \alpha, \beta^{+}, \beta^{+}, \alpha$. Then $\mathrm{Z}$ of the resulting nucleus is
solution:
No. of $\alpha$ particles emitted $=8, \quad$ No. of $\beta^{-}$particles emitted $=4, \quad$ No.of $\beta^{+}$particles emitted $=2$
$z=92-2 \times 8+4-2=78$

subject: Physics
q: In a nuclear fission reaction of an isotope of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>, three similar daughter nuclei of same mass are formed. The speed of a daughter nuclei in terms of mass defect <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>M</mi></math> will be :
question: In a nuclear fission reaction of an isotope of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>, three similar daughter nuclei of same mass are formed. The speed of a daughter nuclei in terms of mass defect <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>M</mi></math> will be :
solution:
Let the reaction be, (P)→(X)+(Y)+(Z)

Let mass of each daughter nuclei be m, then mass defect can be written as ∆M=M-3m.

Now the energy released will be in the form of kinetic energy of the daughter nuclei. Therefore, 

ΔMc2=12mV2+12mV2+12mV2⇒V=2ΔMc23m=2ΔMc2M-∆M≈c2ΔMM

subject: Physics
q: The half life period of a radioactive substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> days. The time taken for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> th of its original mass to disintegrate will be :
question: The half life period of a radioactive substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> days. The time taken for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> th of its original mass to disintegrate will be :
solution:
Given half life period of a radioactive substance is t12=60 days.

Here, 78th of radioactive substance disintegrates means 18th remains or 123.

∴  3 half lives =3×60=180 days

subject: Physics
q: The half life of a radioactive substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> years. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> years a given sample of the radioactive substance gets reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of its initial value. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ____
question: The half life of a radioactive substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> years. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> years a given sample of the radioactive substance gets reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of its initial value. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ____
solution:
6.25% of the initial value means, final number of nuclei should be, N016.

Number of nuclei remaining after n half lives is N=N02n
6.25% N0=N02n
6.25100N0=N02n ⇒625104=12n
⇒2n=10454 ⇒2n=24
⇒n=4

Therefore, time taken will be, n×t12=4×5=20 years

q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The nuclear density of nuclides B510, L36i, F2656e, N1020e and B83209i can be arranged as ρBiN>ρFeN>ρNeN>ρBN>ρLiN

Reason R : The radius R of nucleus is related to its mass number A as R=R0 A13, where R0 is a constant.

In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The nuclear density of nuclides B510, L36i, F2656e, N1020e and B83209i can be arranged as ρBiN>ρFeN>ρNeN>ρBN>ρLiN

Reason R : The radius R of nucleus is related to its mass number A as R=R0 A13, where R0 is a constant.

In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
Nuclear density is given by, ρ=m43πR03, where m is mass of proton. Clearly it is independent of A and same for all nucleus.

Radius of the nucleus is given by, R=R0 A13.

question: A radioactive sample at any instant has its disintegration rate 5000 disintegrations per minute. After $5 \mathrm{~min}-$ utes, the rate is 1250 disintegrations per minute. Then, the decay constant (per minute) is

subject: Physics
q: Mass numbers of two nuclei are in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. Their nuclear densities will be in the ratio of
question: Mass numbers of two nuclei are in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. Their nuclear densities will be in the ratio of
solution:
Radius of nucleus R=R0 A13

Density of nucleus ρ= Mass of nucleus  volume of nucleus 

ρ=m×A43πR3  Where m: mass of proton or neutron and A: mass number.

⇒ρ=m×A43πR0 3A

⇒ρ∝A0

Hence, density of nucleus is independent of mass number.

Therefore, the ratio is 1:1.

question: The intensity of gamma radiation from a given source is I. On passing through $36 \mathrm{~mm}$ of lead, it is reduced to $\frac{I}{8}$. The thickness of lead which will reduce the intensity to $\frac{I}{2}$ will be

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: For all elements, greater the mass of the nucleus, greater is the binding energy per nucleon.
Statement II: For all elements, nuclei with less binding energy per nucleon transforms to nuclei with greater binding energy per nucleon.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: For all elements, greater the mass of the nucleus, greater is the binding energy per nucleon.
Statement II: For all elements, nuclei with less binding energy per nucleon transforms to nuclei with greater binding energy per nucleon.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I is false because binding energy per nucleon does not increase monotonically with mass number. It peaks at Iron-56 and decreases for heavier elements.
Statement II is true. It describes the fundamental principle of nuclear reactions: fission of heavy nuclei (low BE/A) and fusion of light nuclei (low BE/A) both produce nuclei with higher binding energy per nucleon, releasing energy in the process. Both processes are energetically favorable for the same reason: moving toward higher stability (higher BE/A).

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus $n_2$ has 3 times the decay constant as compared to the decay constant of another radioactive nucleus $n_1$. If initial number of both nuclei are the same, what is the ratio of number of nuclei of $n_2$ to the number of nuclei of $n_1$, after one half-life of $n_1$ ?
question: A radioactive nucleus $n_2$ has 3 times the decay constant as compared to the decay constant of another radioactive nucleus $n_1$. If initial number of both nuclei are the same, what is the ratio of number of nuclei of $n_2$ to the number of nuclei of $n_1$, after one half-life of $n_1$ ?
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{N}_2=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-3 \lambda t} \\ & \mathrm{~N}_1=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}_2}{\mathrm{~N}_1}=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \lambda \mathrm{t}} \\ & \mathrm{t}_{\text {half lifeofN }} \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{t}=\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}{ }_{\mathrm{n}} 1 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~N}_0}{2}=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}\end{aligned}$


$\begin{aligned} & \lambda \mathrm{t}=\ln 2 \\ & \mathrm{t}=\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{-2 \lambda \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~N}_2}{\mathrm{~N}_1}=\frac{1}{4}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Assume that a neutron breaks into a proton and an electron. The energy released during this process is (Mass of neutron $=1.6725 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg} ;$ mass of proton $=1.6725 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg} ;$ mass of electron $=9 \times 10^{-31}$ $\mathrm{kg})$
question: Assume that a neutron breaks into a proton and an electron. The energy released during this process is (Mass of neutron $=1.6725 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg} ;$ mass of proton $=1.6725 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg} ;$ mass of electron $=9 \times 10^{-31}$ $\mathrm{kg})$
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\Delta m & =\left(m_p+m_e\right)-m_n \\
& =9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}.
\end{aligned}$
Energy released $=\left(9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right) \mathrm{c}^2$ joules
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{9 \times 10^{-31} \times\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2}{1.6 \times 10^{-13}} \mathrm{MeV} \\
& =0.73 \mathrm{~MeV} .
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nuclei with decay constant $0.5 / \mathrm{s}$ is being produced at a constant rate of 100 nuclei/s. If at $t=0$ there were no nuclei, the time when there are 50 nuclei is:
question: A radioactive nuclei with decay constant $0.5 / \mathrm{s}$ is being produced at a constant rate of 100 nuclei/s. If at $t=0$ there were no nuclei, the time when there are 50 nuclei is:
solution:
Let $N$ be the number of nuclei at any time $t$ then,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{d N}{d t}=100-\lambda N \\
&\text { or } \int_0^N \frac{d N}{(100-\lambda N)}=\int_0^t d t \\
&-\frac{1}{\lambda}[\log (100-\lambda N)]_0^N=t \\
&\log (100-\lambda N)-\log 100=-\lambda t \\
&\log \frac{100-\lambda N}{100}=-\lambda t \\
&\frac{100-\lambda N}{100}=e^{-\lambda t} \\
&1-\frac{\lambda N}{100}=e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&N=\frac{100}{\lambda}\left(1-e^{-\lambda} t\right) \\
&\text { As, } N=50 \text { and } \lambda=0.5 / \mathrm{sec} \\
&\therefore 50=\frac{100}{0.5}\left(1-e^{-0.5 t}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
Solving we get,
$$
t=2 \ln \left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \mathrm{sec}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Two nuclei of mass number $3$ combine with another nucleus of mass number $4$ to yield a nucleus of mass number $10$. If the binding energy per nucleon for the mass numbers $3$, $4$ and $10$ are $5.6$ MeV, $7.4$ MeV and $6.1$ MeV, respectively, then in the process, $\Delta Mc^2 = $ _____ MeV.
question: Two nuclei of mass number $3$ combine with another nucleus of mass number $4$ to yield a nucleus of mass number $10$. If the binding energy per nucleon for the mass numbers $3$, $4$ and $10$ are $5.6$ MeV, $7.4$ MeV and $6.1$ MeV, respectively, then in the process, $\Delta Mc^2 = $ _____ MeV.
solution:
The nuclear reaction is given by:

$2 X^{3} + Y^{4} \rightarrow Z^{10}$

The total binding energy of the reactants is the sum of the binding energies of the two nuclei of mass number $3$ and the nucleus of mass number $4$.

Binding energy of reactants $= 2 \times (3 \times 5.6) + (4 \times 7.4)$

$= 33.6 + 29.6 = 63.2$ MeV

The total binding energy of the product nucleus of mass number $10$ is:

Binding energy of product $= 10 \times 6.1 = 61.0$ MeV

The change in mass energy $\Delta M c^2$ is equal to the difference in the total binding energies of the reactants and the product.

$\Delta M c^2 = |63.2 - 61.0| = 2.2$ MeV

Answer: $2.2$

subject: Physics
q:
The disintegration energy $Q$ for the nuclear fission of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U} \rightarrow{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}+{ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}+n$ is ______ $\mathrm{MeV}$.
Given atomic masses of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}: 235.0439 u ;{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}: 139.9054 u$,
${ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}: 93.9063 u ; n: 1.0086 u \text {, }$
Value of $c^2=931 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{u}$
question:
The disintegration energy $Q$ for the nuclear fission of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U} \rightarrow{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}+{ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}+n$ is ______ $\mathrm{MeV}$.
Given atomic masses of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}: 235.0439 u ;{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}: 139.9054 u$,
${ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}: 93.9063 u ; n: 1.0086 u \text {, }$
Value of $c^2=931 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{u}$
solution:
${ }^{235} \mathrm{U} \rightarrow{ }^{140} \mathrm{Ce}+{ }^{94} \mathrm{Zr}+\mathrm{n}$
Disintegration energy $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Q} & =\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{R}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}\right) \mathrm{c}^2 \\ \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{R}} & =235.0439 \mathrm{u} \\ \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} & =139.9054 \mathrm{u}+93.9063 \mathrm{u}+1.0086 \mathrm{u} \\ & =234.8203 \mathrm{u} \\ \mathrm{Q} & =(235.0439 \mathrm{u}-234.8203 \mathrm{u}) \mathrm{c}^2 \\ & =0.2236 \mathrm{c}^2 \\ & =0.2236 \times 931 \\ \mathrm{Q} & =208.1716 \end{aligned}$

q: Given the masses of various atomic particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0072</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0087</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.000548</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.0141</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>=</mo></math>proton, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>≡</mo></math>neutron, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>≡</mo></math>electron, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover><mo>≡</mo></math>antineutrino and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover><mo>≡</mo></math>deuteron. solution:
For n+p→d+γ,

The mass defect, ∆m=mp+mn-md

⇒∆m=1.0072+1.0087-2.0141

subject: Physics
q:
Assuming the experimental mass of ${}^{12}_{6}C$ as $12\text{ u}$, the mass defect of ${}^{12}_{6}C$ atom is _______ $\text{MeV}/c^2$.
(Mass of proton $= 1.00727\text{ u}$, mass of neutron $= 1.00866\text{ u}$, $1\text{ u} = 931.5\text{ MeV}/c^2$ and $c$ is the speed of the light in vacuum).
question:
Assuming the experimental mass of ${}^{12}_{6}C$ as $12\text{ u}$, the mass defect of ${}^{12}_{6}C$ atom is _______ $\text{MeV}/c^2$.
(Mass of proton $= 1.00727\text{ u}$, mass of neutron $= 1.00866\text{ u}$, $1\text{ u} = 931.5\text{ MeV}/c^2$ and $c$ is the speed of the light in vacuum).
solution:
The mass of $6$ protons is $6 \times 1.00727\text{ u} = 6.04362\text{ u}$.

The mass of $6$ neutrons is $6 \times 1.00866\text{ u} = 6.05196\text{ u}$.

Total mass of the constituent nucleons is $6.04362\text{ u} + 6.05196\text{ u} = 12.09558\text{ u}$.

The mass defect $\Delta m$ is the difference between the total mass of the nucleons and the experimental mass of the atom (ignoring electron mass as per the given data):

$\Delta m = 12.09558\text{ u} - 12\text{ u} = 0.09558\text{ u}$

Converting the mass defect into $\text{MeV}/c^2$:

$\Delta m = 0.09558 \times 931.5\text{ MeV}/c^2 = 89.03277\text{ MeV}/c^2 \approx 89.03\text{ MeV}/c^2$

Answer: $89.03$

subject: Physics
q: Assume that protons and neutrons have equal masses. Mass of a nucleon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and radius of nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The approximate ratio of the nuclear density and water density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>13</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is _____.
question: Assume that protons and neutrons have equal masses. Mass of a nucleon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and radius of nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The approximate ratio of the nuclear density and water density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>13</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
As we know, density of nuclei= mass of nuclei  volume of nuclei 

⇒ρ=1.6×10-27A43π1.5×10-153A

=1.6×10-2714.14×10-45=0.113×1018 kg m-3

We also know that, density of water is ρw=103 kg m-3.

Hence,
ρρw=11.31×1013.

Therefore, n≈11

At the end, there are <math><mn>6</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particles and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>β</mi></math>-particles which are emitted. question: In a radioactive decay chain, the initial nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi> </mi><mn>90</mn><mn>232</mn></msubsup><mi>Th</mi></math>. At the end, there are <math><mn>6</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particles and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>β</mi></math>-particles which are emitted. However, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. Which of the following figures represents the evolution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> with respect to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>?
question: At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a material is composed of two radioactive atoms <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> The decay constant of both kind of radioactive atoms is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. However, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. Which of the following figures represents the evolution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> with respect to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>?
solution:
The rate at which nuclei of B is forming, 

dNBdt=λNA-λNB

dNBdt=2λNB0e-λt-λNB

e-λtdNBdt+λNB=2λNB0e-λteλt

ddtNBe-λt=2λNB0

on integrating above the equation, 
NBeλt=2λtNB+NB0
NBeλt=2λtNB0+NB0

NB=NB1+2λte-λtddtNB=0

-λ1+2λte-λt+2λe-λt=0NBmax at t=12λ

question: Some nuclei of a radioactive material are undergoing radioactive decay. The time gap between the instances when a quarter of the nuclei have decayed and when half of the nuclei have decayed is given as: (where λ is the decay constant)
solution:
3N04=N0e-λt1

N02=N0e-λ2

ln3/4=-λt1 … (1)

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus (initial mass number $A$ and atomic number Z) emits $3 \alpha$-particles and 2 positrons. The ratio of number of neutrons to that of protons in the final nucleus will be
question: A radioactive nucleus (initial mass number $A$ and atomic number Z) emits $3 \alpha$-particles and 2 positrons. The ratio of number of neutrons to that of protons in the final nucleus will be
solution:
In positive beta decay a proton is transformed into a neutron and a positron is emitted.
$$
\mathrm{p}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{n}^0+\mathrm{e}^{+}
$$
no. of neutrons initially was $A-Z$
no. of neutrons after decay $(A-Z)-3 \times 2$ (due to alpha particles) $+2 \times 1$ (due to positive beta decay)
The no. of proton will reduce by 8 . [as $3 \times 2$ (due to alpha particles) $+2$ (due to positive beta decay)] Hence atomic number reduces by 8 .

subject: Physics
q: The decay of a proton to neutron is :
question: The decay of a proton to neutron is :
options: not possible as proton mass is less than the neutron mass
not possible but neutron to proton conversion is possible
always possible as it is associated only with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> decay
solution: It is possible only inside the nucleus and not otherwise as it can not decay to a state of greater mass. Inside a nucleus however, the situation is different, as the binding energy of a nucleus provides the necessary energy to compensate for the neutron-proton mass difference.

subject: Physics
q: The energy equivalent of $1 \mathrm{~g}$ of substance is :
question: The energy equivalent of $1 \mathrm{~g}$ of substance is :
solution:
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{mC}^2$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}=\left(1 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}=\left(10^{-3}\right)\left(9 \times 10^{16}\right)\left(6.241 \times 10^{18}\right) \mathrm{eV} \\ & \mathrm{E}=56.169 \times 10^{31} \mathrm{eV} \\ & \mathrm{E} \approx 5.6 \times 10^{26} \mathrm{MeV} \\ & \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The binding energy per nucleon is practically independent of the atomic number for nuclei of mass number in the range 30 to 170.

Reason R : Nuclear force is short ranged.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The binding energy per nucleon is practically independent of the atomic number for nuclei of mass number in the range 30 to 170.

Reason R : Nuclear force is short ranged.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
The binding energy for stable nuclei is always a positive number, as the nucleus must gain energy for the nucleons to move apart from each other. Nucleons are attracted to each other by the strong nuclear force. Binding energy per nucleon is almost constant in the range of 30-170. This is because nuclear energy is a short range force. It operates in the femto metre region.

Hence, this option is the correct one.

subject: Physics
q:
The average energy released per fission for the nucleus of ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is 190 MeV. When all the atoms of 47 g pure ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ undergo fission process, the energy released is $\alpha \times 10^{23} \mathrm{MeV}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Avogadro Number $=6 \times 10^{23}$ per mole)
question:
The average energy released per fission for the nucleus of ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is 190 MeV. When all the atoms of 47 g pure ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ undergo fission process, the energy released is $\alpha \times 10^{23} \mathrm{MeV}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Avogadro Number $=6 \times 10^{23}$ per mole)
solution:
The number of $^{235}\text{U}$ atoms in 47 g is determined using Avogadro's number.
Molar mass of $^{235}\text{U}$ is 235 g/mol, so the number of moles is $n = 47/235$ mol.
The total number of atoms is $N = (47/235) \times 6 \times 10^{23}$.
Since each fission releases 190 MeV, the total energy released is:
$E_{total} = N \times 190 = \frac{47 \times 6 \times 10^{23}}{235} \times 190 = \frac{47 \times 6 \times 190}{235} \times 10^{23}$ MeV.
Calculating the numerator: $47 \times 6 \times 190 = 282 \times 190 = 53580$.
Therefore: $E_{total} = \frac{53580}{235} \times 10^{23} = 228 \times 10^{23}$ MeV, giving $\alpha = 228$.

subject: Physics
q: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>-value of a nuclear reaction and kinetic energy of the projectile particle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> are related as
question: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>-value of a nuclear reaction and kinetic energy of the projectile particle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> are related as
solution:
The Q-value of the reaction is defined as the difference between the sum of the masses of the initial reactants and the sum of the masses of the final products, in energy units.

For any reaction to happen spontaneously there must be some energy released in the process. Therefore,

Kp+Q>0.

q: A radioactive sample is undergoing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> decay, At any time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> its activity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and another time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> the activity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math>. What is the average life time for the sample ?
question: A radioactive sample is undergoing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> decay, At any time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> its activity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and another time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> the activity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math>. What is the average life time for the sample ?
solution:
Let initial activity be A0

A=A0e-λt      ...1

A5=A0e-λt2     ...2

1÷2

5=eλt2-t1

λ=ln5t2-t1=1τ

τ=t2-t1ln5

subject: Physics
q: Two radioactive substances <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have decay constants <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> respectively. At<math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a sample has the same number of the two nuclei. The time taken for the ratio of the number of nuclei to become <math><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be
question: Two radioactive substances <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have decay constants <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> respectively. At<math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a sample has the same number of the two nuclei. The time taken for the ratio of the number of nuclei to become <math><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be
solution:
Using the decay equation, N=N0e-λt
For radioactive substance A, N1=N0e-5λt ...........(i)
For radioactive substance B, N2=N0e-λt ............(ii)
From equation (i) and (ii)
N1N2=e-5λte-λt
1e2=e-4λt
4λt=2
Time taken, t=12λ

=(13×1.00726+14×1.00866)-27.18846

q:
Two lighter nuclei combine to form a comparatively heavier nucleus by the relation given below:

 X12+X12=Y24

 The binding energies per nucleon X12 and Y24 are 1.1MeV and 7.6MeV respectively. The energy released in this process is_____ MeV.
question:
Two lighter nuclei combine to form a comparatively heavier nucleus by the relation given below:

 X12+X12=Y24

 The binding energies per nucleon X12 and Y24 are 1.1MeV and 7.6MeV respectively. The energy released in this process is_____ MeV.
solution:
Energy released in the given process = Binding energy of product - Binding energy of reactants.

=7.6×4-1.1×2×2

=30.4-4.4

=26MeV

subject: Physics
q:
A common example of alpha decay is

23892U→23490Th+2He4+Q

Given:

23892U=238.05060 u

23490Th=234.04360 u

42He=4.00260 u and 1u=931.5MeVc2

The energy released Q during the alpha decay of 23892U is _____ MeV.
question:
A common example of alpha decay is

23892U→23490Th+2He4+Q

Given:

23892U=238.05060 u

23490Th=234.04360 u

42He=4.00260 u and 1u=931.5MeVc2

The energy released Q during the alpha decay of 23892U is _____ MeV.
solution:
The formula to calculate the Q-value of the given nuclear reaction can be written as

Q=mU-mTh-mHec2   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required Q-value for the reaction.

Q=238.05060 u-234.04360 u-4.00260 uc2=0.0044c2×931.5 MeV/c2≈ 4 MeV

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus can decay by two different processes. Half-life for the first process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> hours while it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> hours for the second process. The effective halflife of the nucleus will be
question: A radioactive nucleus can decay by two different processes. Half-life for the first process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> hours while it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> hours for the second process. The effective halflife of the nucleus will be
solution:
Here the particle is decayed by two processes with decay constant λ1 and λ2.

  

Radioactive decay rate is directly proportional to number of nucleus present at any time, so total rate of decay is 

dAdt--λ1A+-λ2A

⇒dAdt=-λ1+λ2A

⇒λeff =λ1+λ2

⇒ln2t12eff=ln2t121+ln2t122

⇒t12off =4.5×37.5 hours =1.8 hours
